evidence: Query layer uses IOException consistently as the single checked exception type for index-access failures: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery, executeCqlQuery, getTotalFrequency all declare 'throws IOException'
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor wraps all open-index failures: 'throw new IOException("Failed to open index: " + e.getMessage(), e)' — context preserved with cause chained
CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch wraps InvalidQuery: 'throw new IOException("BCQL parse error: " + e.getMessage(), e)' — consistent wrapping pattern
ExplorationHandlers separates IOException (HTTP 500) from IllegalArgumentException (HTTP 400) at the API boundary — correct semantic classification after recent error-handling fix

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams swallows URL decode failures: 'catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...) }' — the failing key-value pair is silently dropped; caller receives an incomplete parameter map with no indication

ExplorationHandlers uses null-return as an error-signaling mechanism alongside exception throwing: requireParam/resolveRelationConfig/resolveExploreParams return null and send the HTTP error internally, then callers check 'if (x == null) return' — this is a second error channel running parallel to exceptions
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern catches nothing; throws IOException and IllegalArgumentException — consistent with the query layer contract
Main.java catches the generic Exception at the top-level switch: 'catch (Exception e) { logger.error("Application error", e); }' — acceptable as a last-resort handler at the CLI entry point
GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException with specific messages for each validation failure (missing version, missing id, duplicate id, etc.) — good context preservation throughout the parsing path

CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates calls getTotalFrequency per collocate in a loop, each potentially throwing IOException — no batching or partial-failure handling; one failed frequency lookup aborts the entire ranking

strengths: IOException is used uniformly as the checked boundary type across the entire query layer — no mix of checked/unchecked or multiple competing exception types
All BlackLab exception wrapping preserves the original cause via chained exceptions — context is not lost at the library boundary
API handlers correctly distinguish 400 (bad input) from 500 (execution failure) with separate catch blocks — the recent fix for HTTP error misclassification is reflected
GrammarConfigLoader uses targeted IOException messages that identify the specific config error by field/index — actionable error context for operators
error_consistency.issue_character: Two structural inconsistencies: a null-return error channel in handlers that runs parallel to the exception channel used elsewhere, and a silent swallow of URL-decode failures in parseQueryParams; both are contained to the API layer but affect predictability.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query and config layers have strong, consistent error handling: IOException as the single checked type, cause-chained wrapping at library boundaries, and specific messages throughout. The API layer introduces two inconsistencies that pull the score down: parseQueryParams silently drops malformed query parameters (callers can't distinguish 'param missing' from 'param malformed'), and the null-return pattern used by requireParam/resolveRelationConfig/resolveExploreParams runs as a parallel non-exception error channel alongside the IOException approach used everywhere else. Neither is catastrophic, but they create predictability debt for developers extending the handler layer.

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams silently drops malformed URL-encoded parameters, returning an incomplete map with no indication of the parse failure
evidence: parseQueryParams: 'catch (Exception e) { logger.warn("Failed to decode query parameter '{}': {}", pair, e.getMessage(), e); }' — loop continues after the warning
The resulting params map silently omits the malformed key; requireParam will then report 'Missing required parameter' rather than 'Malformed parameter value', obscuring the real cause
All four exploration handlers call parseBaseExploreRequest which delegates to this method without any way to detect partial parse failures
issues.suggestion: When a parameter decode fails, either include the raw (un-decoded) value in the map under its raw key, or propagate a warning to the caller via a separate return type (e.g. a record containing the params map and a list of decode errors). At minimum, log the full pair and consider returning an error response if the failing key matches a required parameter.

issues.summary: Handler helper methods (requireParam, resolveRelationConfig, resolveExploreParams) signal errors via null return plus side-effectful HTTP response, creating a dual error channel alongside the IOException pattern used elsewhere
evidence: HttpApiUtils.requireParam: returns @Nullable String; 'if (v.isEmpty()) { sendError(exchange, 400, ...); return null; }'
ExplorationHandlers: 'String seed = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(...); if (seed == null) return;' — null-return check required at every call site
resolveRelationConfig and resolveExploreParams follow the same side-effectful null-return pattern
The query layer exclusively uses exceptions; this null-return style is an inconsistent error-propagation mechanism confined to the API layer
issues.suggestion: Consider extracting a sealed result type or checked exception for handler-level validation failures, so the error channel is uniform. If null-return is kept for brevity, at minimum document the contract clearly at the method level and ensure all handlers consistently follow the 'if (x == null) return' pattern (currently they do, but the pattern is fragile for new contributors).

root_causes: The null-return pattern in handlers was likely chosen to avoid checked exceptions at the HTTP layer, but the resulting dual-channel design was not standardised with a documented pattern
parseQueryParams uses a broad catch to tolerate malformed client input, but the silent-drop semantics were not reconsidered when requireParam was introduced as the downstream caller
likely_symptoms: query_param_silent_swallow can cause misleading 'Missing required parameter' errors when the actual problem is URL encoding
possible_false_positives: Main.java top-level Exception catch is an intentional last-resort handler — not an error_consistency defect
